lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: Neighbour, well met: whither away so fast?

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: I promise you, I scarcely know myself:

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: Hear you the news abroad?

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: Ay, that the king is dead.

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: Bad news, by’r lady; seldom comes the better:

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: I fear, I fear ’twill prove a troublous world.

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: Neighbours, God speed!

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: Give you good morrow, sir.

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: Doth this news hold of good King Edward’s death?

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: Ay, sir, it is too true; God help the while!

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: Then, masters, look to see a troublous world.

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: No, no; by God’s good grace his son shall reign.

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: Woe to the land that’s govern’d by a child!

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: In him there is a hope of government,

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: That in his nonage council under him,

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: And in his full and ripen’d years himself,

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: No doubt, shall then and till then govern well.

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: So stood the state when Henry the Sixth

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: Was crown’d in Paris but at nine months old.

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: Stood the state so? No, no, good friends, God wot;

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: For then this land was famously enrich’d

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: With politic grave counsel; then the king

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: Had virtuous uncles to protect his grace.

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: Why, so hath this, both by the father and mother.

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: Better it were they all came by the father,

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: Or by the father there were none at all;

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: For emulation now, who shall be nearest,

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: Will touch us all too near, if God prevent not.

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: O, full of danger is the Duke of Gloucester!

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: And the queen’s sons and brothers haught and proud:

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: And were they to be ruled, and not to rule,

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: This sickly land might solace as before.

lines.speaker: FIRST CITIZEN
lines.text: Come, come, we fear the worst; all shall be well.

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: When clouds appear, wise men put on their cloaks;

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: When great leaves fall, the winter is at hand;

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: When the sun sets, who doth not look for night?

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: Untimely storms make men expect a dearth.

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: All may be well; but, if God sort it so,

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: ’Tis more than we deserve, or I expect.

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: Truly, the souls of men are full of dread:

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: Ye cannot reason almost with a man

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: That looks not heavily and full of fear.

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: Before the times of change, still is it so:

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: By a divine instinct men’s minds mistrust

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: Ensuing dangers; as, by proof, we see

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: The waters swell before a boisterous storm.

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: But leave it all to God. Whither away?

lines.speaker: SECOND CITIZEN
lines.text: Marry, we were sent for to the justices.

lines.speaker: THIRD CITIZEN
lines.text: And so was I: I’ll bear you company.

glosses.definition: by Our Lady — a mild oath, invoking the Virgin Mary
rarely does something better follow — proverbial
God help us in the meantime

glosses.phrase: Woe to the land that’s govern’d by a child
glosses.definition: Ecclesiastes 10:16, 'Woe to thee, O land, when thy king is a child'

glosses.phrase: Henry the Sixth / Was crown’d in Paris but at nine months old
glosses.definition: Henry VI was crowned infant king of both England (1422) and France (1431) — the citizen's historical analogy

glosses.phrase: When clouds appear, wise men put on their cloaks
glosses.definition: the Third Citizen's proverb-cluster; Shakespeare gives the common reader the playwright's own foreshadowing

glosses.phrase: Untimely storms make men expect a dearth
glosses.definition: storms out of season signal a coming famine — political weather-lore
summoned to the justices of the peace — routine citizen duty

annotations.title: The choric citizens: ordinary voices in an extraordinary play
annotations.body: The three citizens' scene is Shakespeare's equivalent of the commoner-chorus he will deploy more elaborately in Julius Caesar (the Roman crowds) and Coriolanus. Here it is compact and haunted. The Third Citizen's cluster of weather-proverbs — "When clouds appear, wise men put on their cloaks; / When great leaves fall, the winter is at hand; / When the sun sets, who doth not look for night?" — operates as choric foreknowing, the ordinary man reading political weather as climate. The scene does critical structural work: it establishes that Richard's rise is not invisible to ordinary citizens. They see it coming. They do not know what to do. The play has just moved from Edward's death (2.1) to the wailing queens (2.2) to street-level fear, and in three short movements it has represented the whole polity grieving.
sources: Andrew Gurr, Playgoing in Shakespeare's London (1987)

annotations.title: "Woe to the land that's govern'd by a child"
annotations.body: The First Citizen's quotation — "Woe to the land that's govern'd by a child" — is Ecclesiastes 10:16 in the Geneva Bible's phrasing ("Woe be unto thee, O land, when thy King is a child"). The citation is not incidental. Elizabethan political theology, especially in periods of anxious succession, treated the question of the underage king with great seriousness: Henry VI's own nine-month coronation is brought up here, and the Third Citizen's response (that Henry VI's reign had "virtuous uncles" to protect him, unlike Edward V's) names the exact political anxiety that Richard will exploit. The scene offers the audience not Richard's propaganda but its critical reception: the citizens understand why Richard could claim the protectorate and why that claim is dangerous. Shakespeare is letting his stage show his audience what his audience would have recognized as their own political literacy.
sources: John Jowett, The Oxford Shakespeare: Richard III (2000)
Blair Worden, The Sound of Virtue: Philip Sidney's Arcadia (1996)

trials.prompt: What role do the three citizens serve in the play's structure?
options: Comic relief with no relation to the main plot
A choric function: ordinary voices reading political weather, establishing that Richard's rise is visible to citizens
A contrived obstacle to the main plot
A device to introduce historical backstory the audience has missed

trials.prompt: The First Citizen quotes "Woe to the land that's govern'd by a child." What is the source?
options: Ecclesiastes 10:16 — a biblical commonplace about the political dangers of an underage king
A line from Seneca's Hercules Furens
A Tudor-era statute on royal minority

trials.prompt: The Third Citizen's weather-proverbs ("When clouds appear..." etc.) function as what?
options: A literal forecast of bad weather
Choric foreknowing: ordinary political intuition reading the court as climate
